title.en: Freelance Contract Essentials — Scope, Payment Terms and Getting Paid on Time

answerBox.en: Every freelance engagement needs a written contract fixing: precise scope and revision limits, milestone-linked payments (advance of 25–50% is standard), late-payment interest, IP transfer only on full payment, and termination/kill-fee terms. With it, an unpaid invoice is a clean recovery case — a ₹999 legal notice, then summary suit or MSME Samadhaan if you're Udyam-registered.

en.label: Contract before work starts
en.startsFrom: emails can form it, but sign properly

en.title: Scope in numbers, revisions capped
en.summary: Deliverables, timelines, and what counts as "extra".
en.detail: Write deliverables measurably — "5-page website: home, about, services, blog, contact; 2 revision rounds included; further revisions at ₹X/round". Add client dependencies (content, approvals within N days) so their delays don't become your breach. **Scope creep is the #1 freelance dispute** — the revision cap and change-request pricing clause are what prevent it.

en.title: Payment architecture that protects you
en.summary: Advance + milestones + late interest + kill fee.
en.detail: Standard protective structure: **25–50% advance** before starting, milestone payments on defined deliveries, balance before final handover; invoices due in **15–30 days** with late interest of **1.5–2% per month** stated; a **kill fee** (e.g. 50% of remaining milestone) if the client cancels mid-project. Never deliver final files against a promise — see the IP step.

en.title: IP transfers only on full payment
en.summary: Your leverage clause — the work stays yours until paid.
en.detail: State expressly: **all IP in the deliverables assigns to the client only upon receipt of full payment**; until then you retain ownership and licence nothing. Add your right to portfolio display (unless NDA'd), and moral-rights/credit terms if you care. This clause converts "pay when we feel like it" into "no payment, no rights" — the single strongest lever a freelancer holds.

en.title: When they don't pay: the recovery ladder
en.summary: Reminder → legal notice → summary suit / MSME route.
en.detail: Send a dated payment reminder, then an **advocate's legal notice (₹999)** — most agencies and startups pay on the notice. Escalation: with your written contract + invoices, a **summary suit under Order 37 CPC** (leave-to-defend hurdle for the client); or if you hold **Udyam (MSME) registration**, file on **[MSME Samadhaan](https://samadhaan.msme.gov.in)** — the facilitation council route with compound interest at 3× bank rate under the MSMED Act. Cheque given and bounced? The Section 138 criminal route (see our cheque bounce guide) runs in parallel.

en.footnote: Register on Udyam even as a solo freelancer — service providers qualify as micro enterprises, and the MSMED Act's 45-day payment rule + compound interest is the strongest unpaid-invoice remedy in Indian law.

en.q: The client and I only have emails and WhatsApp — is there a contract?
en.a: Probably yes: offer, acceptance and consideration over email/chat form a contract, and courts accept the trail as evidence. It is just messier to prove scope and terms. Going forward, send a one-page terms note and get a written "confirmed" — or use a proper signed agreement.

en.q: Client took the delivered work and vanished without paying. What now?
en.a: Recovery ladder: reminder, ₹999 advocate notice, then summary suit (your invoices + delivery proof are the written contract Order 37 loves) or MSME Samadhaan if Udyam-registered. If your contract had the IP-on-payment clause, their continued use of the work is also infringement — a separate, powerful pressure point.

en.q: What is a kill fee and is it enforceable?
en.a: A pre-agreed amount payable if the client cancels mid-project — compensating booked time and lost opportunities. As liquidated damages under Section 74 Contract Act, reasonable pre-estimates are enforceable. 25–50% of the unbilled remainder is the common range.

en.q: Can I charge interest on late payments?
en.a: Yes if the contract says so — 1.5–2% per month is standard and enforceable as a contractual term. Udyam-registered freelancers get the statutory override: payment due within 45 days regardless of contract, with compound interest at three times the RBI bank rate on delays.

en.q: Should I work through an LLP/company or as an individual?
en.a: Start individual (with contracts + Udyam); consider an LLP when annual revenue is significant, clients demand entity invoicing, or liability exposure grows (software with damages risk). GST registration triggers at ₹20 lakh services turnover (₹10 lakh special states) or on interstate B2B in some cases — plan invoicing accordingly.

reviewer: Adv. Arjun Nair, Bar Council of Kerala, KER/0973/2012
author: WakilBhai Editorial Team
